coredata.dc:title: A Eu3+-based high sensitivity ratiometric fluorescence sensor for determination of tetracycline combining bi-functional carbon dots by surface functionalization and heteroatom doping
coredata.dc:creator: Yao, Ruihong
Li, Zhijian
Huo, Panpan
Gong, Congcong
Li, Jin
Fan, Congbin
Pu, Shouzhi
coredata.dc:description: A ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu was proposed and used to highly sensitive detection of Tetracycline (TC) residues in complex samples. Combination with surface functionalization of carboxyl group and heteroatom doping of Cu2+, the bi-functional carbon dots (Cu-CDs-COOH) were prepared, which exhibited an outstanding fluorescence properties and optical stability. Thanks to the Cu-CDs-COOH rich in carboxyl groups, Eu3+ can effectively chelate with Cu-CDs-COOH which still maintained excellent optical properties (denoted as Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu). When TC was presented in the sensing system of Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu, the strong blue emission of Cu–COOH was quenched at 467 nm based on inner filter effect while the characteristic peak of Eu3+ at 625 nm was enhanced via antenna effect. Herein, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor was built, which was able to detect TC in the range of 0.4–60 μM with a low limit detection of 36.1 nM. The proposed method also showed a satisfactory result of determination TC reliably and accurately in complex samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Europium
Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Tetracycline
Surface functionalization
Heteroatom doping

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing mechanism of Cu-CDs-COOH-Eu ratiometric sensor toward TC
Fluorescence detection of TC using Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu ratiometric sensor
Selectivity and interference of the Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu ratiometric sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (inset: HRTEM) and corresponding particle size distribution of Cu-CDs-COOH (a) and Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu (b); (c) FT-IR spectra of CDs-COOH, Cu-CDs-COOH and Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu; (d) XPS spectra of Cu-CDs-COOH and Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu.
The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s of Cu-CDs-COOH (a–c) and Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu (d–f), respectively.
(a) UV absorption, fluorescence excitation and fluorescence emission spectra of Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu; (b) Fluorescence spectra of different carbon dots.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of different sensing systems; (b) UV absorption spectrum of TC and fluorescence excitation spectrum of Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu; (c) Fluorescence decay curves of Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu at 467 nm under treated with TC from 0 to 30 μM; (d) UV absorption spectra of different sensing systems.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu sensing system with TC concentrations range from 0 to 75 μM (inset: the corresponding fluorescence photos of sensing systems with 0, 0.4, 4, 10, 15, 20, 30, 45, 60 μM of TC); (b) Plot of F625/F467 versus TC concentration from 0 to 75 μM (inset: the corresponding linear relationship from 0.4 to 60 μM).
The ratio of F625/F467 toward various kinds of interfering species in the absence and presence of TC.
Schematic illustration of preparation of Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu and detection mechanism for tetracycline based on the Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu ratiometric fluorescence sensor.
Detection of TC in actual samples by HPLC and Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu ratiometric fluorescence sensor.

head.ce:title: A Eu3+-based high sensitivity ratiometric fluorescence sensor for determination of tetracycline combining bi-functional carbon dots by surface functionalization and heteroatom doping

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangxi Key Laboratory of Organic Chemistry, Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University, Nanchang, 330013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi Key Laboratory of Organic Chemistry
Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangxi Key Laboratory of Organic Chemistry, Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University, Nanchang 330013, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yuzhang Normal University, Nanchang, 330013, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: YuZhang Normal University, Nanchang 330013, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Jiangxi Key Laboratory of Organic Chemistry, Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University, Nanchang, 330013, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi Key Laboratory of Organic Chemistry
Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu was proposed and used to highly sensitive detection of Tetracycline (TC) residues in complex samples. Combination with surface functionalization of carboxyl group and heteroatom doping of Cu2+, the bi-functional carbon dots (Cu-CDs-COOH) were prepared, which exhibited an outstanding fluorescence properties and optical stability. Thanks to the Cu-CDs-COOH rich in carboxyl groups, Eu3+ can effectively chelate with Cu-CDs-COOH which still maintained excellent optical properties (denoted as Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu). When TC was presented in the sensing system of Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu, the strong blue emission of Cu–COOH was quenched at 467 nm based on inner filter effect while the characteristic peak of Eu3+ at 625 nm was enhanced via antenna effect. Herein, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor was built, which was able to detect TC in the range of 0.4–60 μM with a low limit detection of 36.1 nM. The proposed method also showed a satisfactory result of determination TC reliably and accurately in complex samples.

Using an environmentally friendly method, the bi-functional carbon dots were easily synthesized by surface functionalization of carboxyl group and heteroatom doping of Cu2+, which have outstanding optical properties and stability. Thanks to the Cu-CDs-COOH rich in carboxyl groups, Eu3+ can effectively chelate with Cu-CDs-COOH. A Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu ratiometric fluorescence sensor was successfully proposed and applied for detection of tetracycline (TC). In the present of TC, the strong blue fluorescence emission of Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu at 467 nm was quenched due to inner filter effect (IFE), while Eu3+ was sensitized by the TC (antenna effect, AE) that red fluorescence emission of Eu3+ at 626 nm can be observed.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Due to low production cost and excellent properties in prevention and treatment diseases, tetracycline (TC) has been extensively used in the field of livestock farming, aquaculture, and healthcare products [1]. Notably, the abuse of TC can cause a series of problems, including threats to human health, causing the food safety issue, increasing the rise of dry resistance of bacteria, damaging the environment, etc. [2]. Thus, it is especially necessary to establish a quick and effective method for tracing TC residue in water environment and animal products.

At present, varies of analytical strategies have been used for the sensitive determination of TC, including electrochemical analysis [3], chromatographic analysis [4], colorimetric analysis [5], chemiluminiscence [6] and so on. However, most of these methods may suffer from the disadvantages of complex operation, expensive and sophisticated equipment, complex sample pretreatment processes and time-consuming [7]. In contrast, fluorescent measurement can realize high sensitivity, easy operation, high selectivity and fast response time to detect TC in complex samples [8]. Especially, the ratiometric fluorescence sensors, collecting the variation of two well-resolved wavelengths, can greatly reduce the interference of external factors and improve accuracy because of the self-calibration [9]. Moreover, the ratiometric fluorescent sensor usually can be applied to build visual platforms to detect TC by naked eyes. Thus, a new method for detection of TC based on ratiometric fluorescence sensor, with dual or multiple response signals but easy to establish, is highly desired.

To date, many fluorescence sensors combined with lanthanide ions have been reported for detection of TC, such as DPA-Ce-GMP-Eu ratiometric sensor [7], Eu/CdTe QDs ratiometric sensor [10], GQDs-Eu3+ ratiometric sensor [11]. Though TC (having a β-diketone structure) can effectively sensitize Eu3+ luminescence via AE, water molecules usually will cause the fluorescence quenching during the chelation process of TC and Eu3+ due to high frequency vibrational coupling of O–H bonds [12]. Luckily, ancillary ligands, such as citrate [13], acetylacetone [14], 2-thenoyltrifluoroacetone [15], hydrogen peroxide [16], can be selected to replace water molecules that coordinate with Eu3+. Eu3+-ancillary ligand complex, however, is difficult to establish highly sensitive ratiometric fluorescence sensor for determination of TC. It is necessary to combine another excellent fluorescence signal as a reference or response signal to construct a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on Eu3+-complex for TC detection.

Remarkably, featuring outstanding advantages of outstanding luminescence, low toxicity, unique biocompatibility, good stability and water solubility, carbon dots (CDs) or functional CDs has attracted widespread concerns for application in the sensor field [17], such as using DNA-CuNC/CDs to visual detection of arginine and acetaminophen [18], combining dual-emitting CDs and AgNPs to determination of cymoxanil [19], using dual-emitting CDs/SiDs hybrids to visually monitor ciprofloxacin [20]. To prepare high quality of CDs and further improve the sensing sensitivity, surface functionalization has been proved to be an efficient strategy for achievement the multifunctional CDs [21]. Generally speaking, CDs can be modified with carboxyl groups, amino groups, and hydroxyl groups. All of these groups are beneficial to increase quantum yield, enhance photostability, reduce cytotoxicity and improve water stability of CDs [22]. Moreover, carboxyl group functionalized CDs are easy to coordinate with metal ions [23]. Thus, the functionalized CDs have enormous advantages in constructing CDs-Eu-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor, where CDs fluorescence intensity can be quenched based on inner filter effect (IEF) and Eu3+can be sensitized by TC. In addition, CDs doped by heteroatom (metallic and nonmetallic elements), which is a powerful strategy for improving quantum yield and adjusting the intrinsic structure of CDs easily [24]. So far, there were various nonmetallic elements doped CDs, mainly including N, P, S, F, B, Si, which were widely used in fluorescent sensors [25–30]. Unlike the CDs doped with nonmetallic elements, metal doping has a great ability to provide more active sties and improve the performance of CDs due to metal ions have more unoccupied orbitals, larger atomic radius and more electrons [31]. CDs doped with Fe [32], Zn [33], Mn [34], Gd [35], La [36], Cu [37] have been prepared and applied in fluorescent sensors. Compared to other metal ions, copper ion is more safety, easy reacting with chemical groups on CDs [37]. Meantime, the CDs doped with copper element can extremely increase the quantum yield of CDs [38]. However, it is still a challenge to prepare high quantum yield of Cu-CDs and investigate its fluorescence properties and application in detection of target analyte.

Herein, a bi-functionalized CDs with excellent luminescence performance (Cu-CDs-COOH), functionalized carboxyl group and doped copper element, was easily synthesis by an environmentally friendly process. Thanks to the Cu2+ and carboxyl group doped on CDs, Cu-CDs-COOH displayed a strong emission at 467 nm (λex = 355 nm). After adding TC, the fluorescence intensity of Cu-CDs-COOH was markedly quenched on account of IFE. Besides, TC can sensitize Eu3+ owing to AE, and the carboxyl group on the surface of Cu-CDs-COOH can chelate with Eu3+ to replace the water molecules, avoiding Eu3+ fluorescence quenching. Moreover, the proposed method exhibited an enormous potential to be applied in visualization detection of TC. Therefore, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor (Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu) was established, combined with bi-functional CDs and Eu3+, and used for determination of TC in complex samples with excellent selectivity and high sensitivity.

ce:para.ce:bold: 2 Experimental sections (showed in the Supplementary material)

ce:section.ce:para: The preparation process of Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu was clearly exhibited in Scheme 1 . Firstly, L-Tryptophan was used as the raw material for the synthesis of CDs, and CuCl2·H2O and citric acid were served as surface functionalized and heteroatom doping reagent, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the prepared Cu-CDs-COOH had a uniform spherical morphology with an average size of 2.17 ± 0.01 nm. The HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 1a) exhibited that the Cu-CDs-COOH revealed the clear lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponded to the plane (100) of graphite and certified the existence of graphite-like crystalline structure in Cu-CDs-COOH [27]. Due to Eu3+ can efficiently chelate with carboxyl groups on the surface of Cu-CDs-COOH, the Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu displayed a larger average diameter (16.31 ± 0.2 nm) than Cu-CDs-COOH (Fig. 1b), proving the successful coordination of Cu-CDs-COOH and Eu3+.

To characterize the chemical compositions and further prove the successful preparation of Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu, the FT-IR spectrum was carried out. As displayed in Fig. 1c, the bonds of O–H/N–H (3000-3500 cm−1), C–N (1428 cm−1), C–O (1232 cm−1) and the groups of C=O (1721 cm−1), C=C (∼1620 cm−1) were observed in CDs-COOH (black line), which indicated that amine and carboxyl groups was recorded in CDs-COOH [39,40]. After doping Cu2+ in CDs-COOH, the absorption peak of N–H (3000-3500 cm−1) was disappeared and the stretching vibration of C=O, C–N and C–O moved to 1730, 1401 and 1222 cm−1 due to Cu2+ can chelate with N and O in CDs-COOH, respectively (red line in Fig. 1c). Besides, a series of characteristic absorption peaks of metal-sensitive bonds appeared in the range of 870–1100 cm−1 for Cu-CDs-COOH, which can belong to Cu–N [37]. Compared with the FT-IR spectra of CDs-COOH and Cu-CDs-COOH, the absorption peaks of N–H, C=O, C–N, C–O at in Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu exhibited a markedly changes. The results showed that Eu3+ can coordinate with CDs which was rich in –COOH.

The XPS spectrum was further identified the successful synthesis of Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu. As displayed in Fig. 1d, the Cl 2p, C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and Cu 2p were clearly observed from Cu-CDs-COOH (black line), corresponding to the binding energies of 199, 285, 401, 532, 934 eV, individually. The Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu not only had the same elements, namely C, N, O and Cu, as Cu-CDs-COOH but also the Eu 3d characteristic peaks were obviously found in the survey of Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu, demonstrating that Eu3+ and Cu-CDs-COOH was successfully coordinated (red line in Fig. 1d). In addition, the C 1s XPS spectrum of Cu-CDs-COOH was deconvoluted into three characteristic peaks at 288.45, 285.03, 284.06 eV, attributing to C=O, C–N/C–O and C–C/C=C bonds, respectively (in Fig. 2 a). The spectrum of N 1s was divided into two peaks (401.03 and 99.39 eV) which indicated the pyrrolic N and pyridinic N existing in Cu-CDs-COOH (in Fig. 2b). As shown in Fig. 2c, the peaks at 532.71 and 531.43 were belong to C=O and C–OH in O 1s spectrum of Cu-CDs-COOH, respectively. Moreover, as Fig. S1 shown of the high-resolution Cu 2p spectrum of Cu-CDs-COOH, the mainly peaks at 953.99 and 933.93 eV were linked to Cu2+ 2p 1/2 and Cu2+ 2p 3/2, individually [41,42]. And, the lower binding energy peaks at 951.49 eV (Cu 2p 1/2) and 931.73 eV (Cu 2p 3/2) were attributed to Cu+ [43]. Besides, as seen in Fig. S2, the valence state of Eu in Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu was mainly Eu3+, which corresponded to Eu3+ 3d 3/2 (1164.88 eV) and Eu3+ 3d 5/2 (1135.33 eV). The binding energy at 1155.21 and 1125.22 eV was assigned to their shake-down satellites, while the peak at 1143.70 eV was attributed to the reduction of Eu3+ into Eu2+ because of charge compensation in the XPS measurement [10,44]. Compared with O 1s (or C 1s) between Cu-CDs-COOH and Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu, the binding energy of C=O had increased from 532.72 eV (or 288.45 eV) to 533.37 eV (or 288.55 eV) due to the fact that the reduction in the electron density of O after Eu3+ chelated with –COOH on Cu-CDs-COOH (Fig. 2a and 2d, Fig. 2c and 2f) [45]. Moreover, a new peak at 531.13 eV was attributed to Eu–O bond (Fig. 2f) [46]. All the results above clearly illustrated that CDs was successfully doped with heteroatom (Cu2+) and functionalized with carboxyl group which can coordinate with Eu3+.

The optical properties of Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu were investigated by UV–vis absorption and fluorescent spectra analysis. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the UV–vis absorption of Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu displayed two absorption peaks at 276 and 351 nm, which were assigned to the π-π* transition of aromatic C=C bond and n-π* transition of the C=O bond, respectively [26]. Under the optimal excitation of 355 nm (showing in Fig. S3), a strong emission peak at 467 nm can be observed (blue line in Fig. 3a). Compared with CDs-COOH and Cu-CDs, Cu-CDs-COOH showed an excellent luminescence property because the surface functionalization and heteroatom doping can effectively create new surface states, influence surface energy traps and electron-hole recombination (in Fig. 3b curve 1–3) [22,47–49]. Moreover, the quantum yield of Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu (12.3%) was apparently higher than CDs-COOH (7.4%) or Cu-CDs (9.7%). After Cu-CDs-COOH coordinated with Eu3+, Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu still exhibited an outstanding fluorescence (Fig. 3b curve 4).

Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu in different systems with different pH from 5.0 to 8.0 had a significant decreased owing to the pyrrole N, pyridinic N and surface groups on Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu can be protonated or deprotonated (in Fig. S4 (a)) [39,50]. There were showing a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu and pH from 5.0 to 8.0 with a linear regression equation of FL = - 458.4 pH + 4776.4 (inset of Fig. S4a). The fluorescence intensity of Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu was also recorded under different conditions, including UV and xenon arc irradiation time, ionic strength. As shown in Fig. S4 (b-d), Cu-CDs-COOH remained outstanding optical properties despite the salt concentration up to 100 mM and kept a good stability after light radiation for 2 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of Cu-CDs-COOH-Eu ratiometric sensor toward TC
ce:section.ce:para: Interestingly, compared with curve 1 and curve 2 in Fig. 4 a, Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu displayed a strong fluorescence emission peak at 467 nm, while the characteristic peak of Eu3+ at 625 nm was obviously enhanced and the emission peak at 467 nm was quenched in the present of TC (λex = 355 nm). But, when TC was added to the systems of Cu-CDs-Eu and Eu3+, neither of them was as specific as Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu (Fig. 4a curve 3–6). Therefore, a Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu ratiometric sensor was proposed for monitoring of TC.

To illustrate the response mechanism of Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu sensor for TC, several test techniques were carried out. As shown in Fig. 4b, TC showed a wide absorption band between 230 nm and 430 nm, which overlapped with the excitation spectrum of Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu (250–400 nm), suggesting that the fluorescence of Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu may be quenched by TC via IFE or Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [13]. Owing to the fluorescence lifetime of Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu displayed a slight variation at different concentration of TC, the quenching mechanism can be proved to be IFE (Fig. 4c and Table S1). Besides, the value of Kq (quenching rate constant) was 5.427 × 1011 M−1 s−1, which was greater than 1.0 × 1010 M−1 s−1, indicating that the quenching mechanism was static quenching (Fig. S5).

In addition, because of having a β-diketone structure and showing a large molar extinction coefficient, TC can chelate with Eu3+ and was usually served as an ideal sensitizer to sensitize Eu3+ based on AE [12]. As shown in Fig. 4d curve 1 to 3, the characteristic absorption peak of TC at 373 nm moved to 400 nm after chelated with Eu3+. Likewise, compared with the sensing system of Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu, the absorption peak of TC was recorded and obviously red shifted when adding the TC to the sensing system (curve 3 and 4 in Fig. 4d). Besides, following the test of zeta potential measurement (Fig. S6), the Cu-CDs-COOH showed a negatively charged, while the zeta potentials of the Cu-CDs-COOH was changed from −23.1 mV to −17.8 mV after the introduction of Eu3+. Moreover, when adding TC to Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu, the zeta potentials of sensing system was further increased to −15.9 mV due to the hydroxyl groups in TC can neutralize negative charge on the surface of carbon dots that the distance between the Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu and TC can be shortened via electrostatic interaction [51]. The result indicated that TC can successfully chelate with Eu3+. Besides, following the singlet excited-state energy (S1 = 36,200 cm−1) and triplet excited-state energy (T1 = 19,160 cm−1) of TC, the energy gap between S1 and T1 of TC was calculated to be 17,040 cm−1 (>5000 cm−1) that the intersystem crossing process between S1 and T1 can favorably occur based on Reinhoudt's empirical rule [52,53]. Moreover, the T1 of TC was higher than the 5D0 level of Eu3+ (17,500 cm−1), suggesting that the high-efficiency energy transfer from TC to Eu3+ (vividly showing in Fig. S7). All the specific data and experimental result can demonstrate that TC can serve as an “antenna” to sensitize Eu3+ luminescence based on AE. All the analysis manifested that TC can efficiently chelate with the coordination unsaturation of Eu3+ on Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu and effectively sensitize Eu3+ due to AE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of TC using Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu ratiometric sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To realize highly sensitive determination of TC for the proposed method, the sensing conditions was investigated. As displayed in Fig. S8 (a), the concentration of Eu3+ used for coordination with Cu-CDs-COOH was optimized, because it played a crucial role in detection sensitivity for TC. The ratio of F625/F467 was almost constant with the Eu3+ concentration from 6 to 24 mM in the absence of TC. In the present of TC, the ratio of F625/F467 reached a maximum when Eu3+ was 18 mM. Considering the detection of sensitivity, 18 mM Eu3+ was chosen to establish the Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu sensor. Due to the fluorescence intensity of Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu was sensitive to pH, the condition of reaction pH value was investigated as well. As described in Fig. S8 (b), the ratio of F625/F467 gradually increased with the increasing of pH range 5.0–8.5 and reached maximum at pH 8.5. To avoid Eu3+ forming europium hydroxide precipitation in strong alkaline conditions and TC being protonated at acidic conditions [54], the weakly alkaline (pH 8.5) sensing condition was conductive to the formation of the Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu-TC, and achieved highly sensitive determination of TC. And, this ratiometric sensor showed a fast response rate within 60 s and became stable after 6 min for detection of TC (Fig. S8 (c)).

Under optimal conditions, the performance of Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu ratiometric sensor for TC determination was assessed. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence intensity of Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu at 467 nm was gradually diminished while the characteristic peak of Eu3+ was significantly enhanced with the increasing of TC concentrations range from 0 to 75 μM. Moreover, the corresponding fluorescence color displayed an obviously change from blue to red, showing a great potential application on visual detection of TC. The ratiometric sensor exhibited a good linear relationship (F625/F467 = 0.00776CTC + 0.0832, R2 = 0.9962) of F625/F467 versus TC concentrations between 0.4 and 60 μM with a detection limit of 36.1 nM (S/N = 3). As displayed in Fig. S9, using traditional single signal sensor for detection of TC by “Turn-Off” mode of Cu-CDs-COOH sensor displayed a linear relationship ranging from 1 to 80 μM and the limit detection was 709.9 nM for TC. Compared with the “Turn-Off” sensor for detection of TC, the Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu ratiometric sensor exhibited a sensitive and wider linear range for determination of TC due to self-calibration of ratiometric fluorescence sensor. According to the performance of different analytical methods and fluorescence sensors previously reported for detection of TC (Table S2 and Table S3), the Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu ratiometric sensor showed a superior sensitivity for TC detection relatively. Moreover, the ratiometric Cu-CDs-COOD-Eu sensor also showed a good light stability and salt resistance (Fig. S10).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interference of the Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu ratiometric sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To estimate the selectivity and interference ability of the Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu sensor for detection of TC, metal ions (Ca2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Fe3+), amino acids (Glutathione (Glu), dl-Proline (Pro), l-Tyrosine (Tyr), l-Valine (Val), l-histidine (His), l-Lysine (Lys), L-Tryptophan (Trp)), antibiotics (chloramphenicol (CAP), erythromycin (ERY), streptomycin sulfate (STR), kanamycin (KAN), neomycin (NEO) and other interfering species (Ascorbic acid (AA), dl-Cysteine (Cys), Homocysteine (Hcy), Glutathione (GSH)) were carried out. As shown in Fig. 6 , the signal response of Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu sensor can hardly be influenced by various interfering species in the present of TC or not, suggesting that the Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu ratiometric sensor can highly selectivity detect of TC in despite of the existence of interfering species.

According to the previous reports, TC may be found in animal products or real water sample. Therefore, the proposed sensor was used for detection of TC in river water, milk sample and honey sample using the method of standard addition so as to investigate the practical application of the Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu ratiometric sensor. As clearly shown in Table 1 , the recoveries were ranging from 98.6%–102.0% and the RSD less than 1.96%. Furthermore, there was no obviously difference between the Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu ratiometric sensor and High Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) by the t-test (p > 0.05), indicating that the proposed sensor had reliable accuracy and reliability to monitor tetracycline in real samples.

In summary, the bi-functional carbon dots (Cu-CDs-COOH) was successfully synthesized and exhibited excellent optical properties, optical stability and a remarkable response to pH between 5.0 and 8.0. Thanks to the enrichment of carboxyl group in Cu-CDs-COOH, a sensitive ratiometric fluorescence sensor via Eu3+ chelated with carboxyl group on Cu-CDs-COOH was easily fabricated and applied to detection of TC. The proposed approach displayed a high sensitivity toward TC and satisfactory recoveries (RSD <0.4%) on real samples detection. More attractively, the fluorescent color of sensing systems displayed obviously changes from blue to red which showing a huge potential for visual determination of TC. Thus, the proposed sensor has great application prospects in food security and environmental monitoring.

Ruihong Yao: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Editing. Zhijian Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Panpan Huo: Methodology, Investigation. Congcong Gong: Methodology, Investigation. Jin Li: Methodology, Investigation. Congbin Fan: Resources, Supervision, Validation, Formal analysis. Shouzhi Pu: Resources, Supervision, Investigation, Review, Validation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (41867053), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (20202BAB213018), the Graduate Innovation Special Fund project of Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University (YC2021-S758).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2022.110190.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in sensors for tetracycline antibiotics and their applications
Trends Anal Chem

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu X, Huang D, Lai C, Zeng G, Qin L, Zhang C, Yi H, Li B, Deng R, Liu S, Zhang Y. Recent advances in sensors for tetracycline antibiotics and their applications. Trends Anal Chem 2018;109:260-274.
